name: Raghavendra Tirtha
dates_notes: Maṭha records, royal inscriptions, near-contemporary hagiography.

dates_evidence.description: Royal inscriptions in the Mantrālaya region.
Mādhva maṭha records and near-contemporary hagiography.

core_thesis:
Rāghavendra Tīrtha is the late-classical synthesiser of Mādhva scholasticism. His doctrinal commitments are continuous with Madhva, Jayatīrtha, and Vyāsatīrtha — *svatantra-paratantra* hierarchy, *pañca-bheda*, *viśeṣa* as the device of intra-substantial differentiation, *bimba-pratibimba* as the structure of *jīva*–Viṣṇu dependence, *sākṣi* as a self-luminous *pramāṇa*, the rejection of *mithyātva* under all five *lakṣaṇa*s. The contribution is consolidation through *prakāśa*-commentary (the genre of "illumination" gloss).

The distinctive dialectical innovations of the two principal *Pariṃala*s are specific. On the *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala*: where Jayatīrtha's *vyāpti*-statements were cast in older *prācīna* idiom, Rāghavendra restates them with the *avacchedaka*-conditions and *paryāpti-sambandha* refinements that *Navya-Nyāya* had developed since Jayatīrtha. The same Mādhva position is preserved; what changes is the level of formal explicitness with which it is defended. The *Pariṃala* therefore functions as the school's response to the further developments in *Navya-Nyāya* through Raghunātha Śiromaṇi and Gadādhara on the Naiyāyika side, and through Madhusūdana, Brahmānanda, and the Vivaraṇa-line on the Advaitin side.

On the *Tarka-Tāṇḍava-Pariṃala*: Rāghavendra's hermeneutic move at the *prāmāṇyavāda*-front is to specify, more sharply than Vyāsatīrtha had done, the conditions under which intrinsic validity (*svataḥ-prāmāṇya*) is graspable; the inference is reformulated to block defeaters that the post-Vyāsatīrtha Naiyāyika tradition had begun to press.

Alongside the *prakāśa*-corpus Rāghavendra composed the *Daśa-Avatāra-Stuti* and a substantial *stotra*-literature. The devotional grammar and the scholastic apparatus are continuous: *bhakti* is the means by which *aparokṣa-jñāna* of the *svatantra* Viṣṇu is reached, and the eternally individual *jīva*'s subordination is preserved at every level of the soteriology.

engaged_works.title: Tantra-Dipika
engaged_works.summary:
A *prakāśa*-style commentary on Madhva's *Anuvyākhyāna*, conducted in dialogue with Jayatīrtha's *Nyāya-Sudhā*. The text is third-order: it mediates Madhva and Jayatīrtha and integrates the school's settled responses to Appayya-line and Madhusūdana-line objections raised in the intervening centuries.

Three topic-clusters are particularly developed. The first is the *bheda*-arguments at the opening of *Anuvyākhyāna* 2, where Madhva's *svarūpa-svabhāva* construal of difference is restated and defended against the Vivaraṇa-line objection that *svarūpa-svabhāva* covertly multiplies essences. The second is the *viśeṣa*-discussion, where Rāghavendra specifies the inferential conditions under which *viśeṣa* may be invoked without circularity. The third is the *bimba-pratibimba* arguments, where the asymmetry of the original-and-image relation between Viṣṇu and *jīva* is preserved against the symmetry-readings of competing schools.

The semantic method is hierarchical. At *Tantra-Dīpikā* 1.1, Rāghavendra fixes the rule that scriptural words (whether established elsewhere, in both places, or only elsewhere) converge on Viṣṇu either *sākṣāt* (directly) or *paramparayā* (through succession), where the convergence is established by the predicates that hold of Viṣṇu uniquely. The rule blocks the Advaitic and Śivādvaita strategies that would absorb apparent Vaiṣṇava *śruti* under a different ultimate.

The *Tantra-Dīpikā* remains the standard reference for modern Mādhva exegesis of the *Anuvyākhyāna*.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Sudha-Parimala
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's Nyāya-Sudhā.
engaged_works.summary:
A sub-sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's *Nyāya-Sudhā*, and so a fourth-order text on Madhva's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. The idiom is the *Navya-Nyāya* idiom Vyāsatīrtha had introduced into the school.

The text does two things at once. First, it records the settled Mādhva response to objections developed since Jayatīrtha, in particular the Appayya, Madhusūdana, Brahmānanda, and Vivaraṇa-line attacks on the *Sudhā*'s reconstruction of Madhva's arguments. Where the *Sudhā*'s *anumāna* admits a Madhusūdana-style defeater, Rāghavendra reformulates the *vyāpti* with a tighter *avacchedaka*-condition; where the defeater can be parried by an *upādhi*-test, the test is supplied. Second, it refines the technical apparatus itself: late-classical *Navya-Nyāya*, through Raghunātha Śiromaṇi and his successors, had developed *paryāpti-sambandha* and finer *avacchedaka*-machinery that Vyāsatīrtha had not had. Rāghavendra brings these into the school's exegesis without doctrinal alteration.

The two layers (Jayatīrtha's *prācīna* arguments inside the *Sudhā*, Rāghavendra's *navīna* refinements in the *Pariṃala*) are arranged so that the school's reading remains a single argued position across centuries.

The parallel *Pariṃala* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* (sometimes counted separately, sometimes grouped with this work) performs the same function for the Naiyāyika engagement. The *Pariṃala* is the terminus of the Madhva → Jayatīrtha → Vyāsatīrtha → Rāghavendra commentarial cascade.

engaged_works.title: Tarka-Tandava-Parimala
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Rāghavendra's mature period as the school's response to the developing Navya-Nyāya scholasticism.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Pariṃala on Vyāsatīrtha's Tarka-Tāṇḍava.
engaged_works.summary:
Rāghavendra's *Pariṃala* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tarka-Tāṇḍava*, paralleling on the *tarka*-front what the *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala* does on the *Anuvyākhyāna*-front. The text engages directly with the Gaṅgeśa-line *Navya-Nyāya* tradition.

Five topic-clusters carry the dialectic. The analysis of *vyāpti* (universal concomitance) and its grasp; the *parāmarśa*-structure of *anumāna* (the moment in inference at which the *hetu* is recognised as instantiating the *vyāpti*); the treatment of *upādhi* as defeater; the analysis of *kevalānvayī* (universal-positive) and *kevala-vyatirekī* (universal-negative) inferences; and the *prāmāṇyavāda* (the doctrine of cognitive validity). In each cluster Rāghavendra works through Vyāsatīrtha's critique of the corresponding Naiyāyika position and brings the school's response up to date with the further developments since the early sixteenth century — Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's *Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa* and Gadādhara's *vāda*-corpus had reset the technical baseline.

The *prāmāṇyavāda* is the locus of one of Rāghavendra's specific dialectical innovations. Vyāsatīrtha had defined intrinsic validity (*svatastva*) at the level of origination as *jñāna-janaka-mātrādhīna-janmatva* — "origination dependent solely on the producer of the cognition." Rāghavendra refines the *adhīnatva* (dependence) clause with an *avacchedaka*-condition that blocks a class of post-Vyāsatīrtha Naiyāyika defeaters, while preserving the substantive Mādhva commitment to *svataḥ-prāmāṇya*.

With the *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala*, the work completes the *Pariṃala*-corpus on the school's two principal scholastic fronts.

engaged_works.title: Tatparya-Dipika
engaged_works.summary:
A *prakāśa* on Vyāsatīrtha's *Tātparya-Candrikā*, and so a fifth-order text on the *Brahma-Sūtra*. The full stack runs *sūtra* → Madhva *bhāṣya* → Jayatīrtha *Tattva-Prakāśikā* → Vyāsatīrtha *Tātparya-Candrikā* → Rāghavendra *Tātparya-Dīpikā*.

The text functions in two registers. As running commentary, it documents the school's settled construal of each contested *adhikaraṇa* of the *Sūtra* in light of the full polemical history through the early seventeenth century. As digest, it shows how the prior commentarial layers cohere as a single argued reading rather than as four superimposed glosses.

Three *adhikaraṇa*s receive particular attention. The *ānanda-mayādhikaraṇa* (1.1.12–19): Vyāsatīrtha had restated the realist reading of *ānandamaya* as Viṣṇu in *Navya-Nyāya* form; Rāghavendra glosses the refinement and shows that it preserves Jayatīrtha's earlier *prācīna* argument intact. The *jīva-pratibimba* discussion at *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43–53, where the *aṃśa-adhikaraṇa* and the difference-*sūtras* of 2.3 together fix the *bimba-pratibimba* relation: the late-classical reading is settled here. The *bheda*-*sūtras* of *adhyāya* 4, where the asymmetry of *jīva*–Viṣṇu dependence is preserved through the *mokṣa*-discussion against any reading that would collapse the dependence in liberation.

With the *Tantra-Dīpikā* and the *Pariṃala*, the *Tātparya-Dīpikā* completes the late-Mādhva commentarial architecture on the *Brahma-Sūtra*.

engaged_works.title: Multiple Prakasha sub-commentaries
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary corpus on the Madhva–Jayatīrtha tradition.
engaged_works.summary:
Beyond the *Tantra-Dīpikā*, *Pariṃala*, and *Tātparya-Dīpikā*, Rāghavendra composed a wider corpus of *prakāśa* sub-commentaries on prior Mādhva texts. The corpus includes glosses on Madhva's *Daśa-Prakaraṇa* (the *Tattvodyota*, the *Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*, *Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana*, and *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana* triad), on the *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya* and *Karma-Nirṇaya*, on the *Gītā-Bhāṣya* and *Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, on portions of the *Bhāgavata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* and *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya*, and on several of Jayatīrtha's smaller *ṭīkā*s.

The unifying intent is integrative. Each text is brought into a reading consistent with the spine of the principal *Pariṃala*-*Dīpikā* productions, so that the school's later exegete encounters not three or five separate Madhva-Jayatīrtha-Vyāsatīrtha layers but a single resolved position. Where prior commentary admits multiple construals, the *prakāśa*-gloss fixes one; where the technical apparatus has moved on since the underlying text was written, the gloss imports the new apparatus without altering the position.

The *prakāśa*-corpus, the *stotra*-literature, and Rāghavendra's pontifical-pedagogical role together constitute the channel through which *Tattvavāda* enters its modern reception. Twentieth-century Dvaita exegesis works downstream of these glosses.

key_passages.locus_long: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1 summary on the convergence of Vedic language on Viṣṇu
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
te ca śabdā anyatra-prasiddhā ubhayatra-prasiddhā anyatraiva-prasiddhāś ca
sākṣāt paramparayā vā viṣṇum eva sṛṣṭy-ādi-vartṛtva-sādhya-guṇa-pūrṇatvena sādhayanti

pada_analysis.gloss: by the state of being full of qualities established through creatorship and the rest

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: sṛṣṭyādibhiḥ vartṛtvaṃ sādhyate ye guṇāḥ, taiḥ pūrṇatvam

karaka_structure.note: The words are the proving subject.
Secondary or mediated signification is marked instrumentally.
The entity established by the words.
The proving proceeds by Viṣṇu's quality-fullness as evidenced by creatorship and allied functions.

key_passages.english_close: And those words — whether established elsewhere, established in both places, or established only elsewhere. They prove Viṣṇu alone, either directly or through paramparā. They do so by [showing him] to be full of qualities established through creatorship and the rest of the [divine] functions.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Rāghavendra's hermeneutic claim is that the Veda's diverse word-stock ultimately converges on Viṣṇu, sometimes immediately and sometimes by mediated indication.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the supreme independent Lord in Dvaita

key_passages.why_this_passage: This adhyāya-summary compresses Rāghavendra's mature semantic method: scriptural language, even when ordinarily used elsewhere, is drawn back to Viṣṇu by a hierarchy of direct and indirect signification. Because his late scholastic authority is fundamentally commentarial, this is more representative than selecting an isolated devotional line.
key_passages.source_edition: Rāghavendra Tīrtha, Tantra Dīpikā, ed. R. S. Panchamukhi (1964); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/raghavendra_tantra_dipika_archive.pdf

key_passages.locus_long: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 1, discussion of the ānandamayādhikaraṇa under the sūtra bhedavyapadeśāc ca
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ānandamayo netara iti vartate |
ānandamayād bhedavyapadeśāc cānandamayo netara ity arthaḥ |
"sa eko brahmaṇa ānandaḥ" ityādinā viriñcadeḥ parimitānandam uktvā |
"yato vāco nivartante" ity ānandamayasya pūrṇānandatvokter iti bhāvaḥ ||

pada_analysis.gloss: because of the predication of difference
because of the statement of full blisshood

karaka_structure.note: The quoted words are the subject of nivartante.
The source from which speech returns.
Difference is predicated from the ānandamaya.
The limited bliss belongs below the ānandamaya, not to it.

key_passages.english_close: "Ānandamaya, not the other": that is the construal. Because there is predication of difference from the ānandamaya, the ānandamaya is not the other. By the expression "that is one joy of Brahman" and the like, limited bliss is stated for Viriñci; by "from which words turn back," full blisshood is stated of the ānandamaya: this is the purport.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Rāghavendra reads the bliss-language of the Upaniṣads as marking a real hierarchy: Brahmā may possess joy, but the ānandamaya designates Viṣṇu's unsurpassed plenitude, not a lower deity or derivative self.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the "bliss-full" one of the Upaniṣadic passage
Brahmā, the high but dependent creator deity

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a representative Rāghavendra move because it shows him securing real hierarchy and real difference through close semantic reading of śruti. It is not merely a famous slogan: the argument turns on how bliss-language is distributed between Viṣṇu and dependent beings, which is central to late Mādhva ontology.
key_passages.source_edition: Rāghavendra Tīrtha, Tantra Dīpikā, ed. R. S. Panchamukhi (1964); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/raghavendra_tantra_dipika_archive.pdf

key_passages.locus_long: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 3, discussion of the sūtra suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na cābhedena sopapattiḥ |
"prājñenātmanā sampariṣvaktaḥ" iti |
"prājñenātmanānvarūḍhaḥ" iti ca |
suṣuptyutkrāntyor bhedena jīveśayor vyapadeśa iti paramparayā yojyam ||

pada_analysis.gloss: of the jīva and the Lord

karaka_structure.note: Rejected explanatory instrument: non-difference does not account for the passage.
The states in which the distinction is read.
The designation belongs to the pair jīva / Īśvara.
The exegetical connection is sequential rather than by identity.

key_passages.english_close: And it is not tenable on the assumption of non-difference. [The śruti says]: "Embraced by the prājña-self". And [the śruti also says]: "mounted upon the prājña-self". Thus, in deep sleep (suṣupti) and in departure (utkrānti), there is designation of the jīva and the Lord as different. So the connection is to be made by sequential linkage (paramparā).
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Rāghavendra insists that the deep-sleep and death-transition texts preserve dependence without identity: the self is related to the Lord, but not collapsed into him.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the higher conscious principle invoked in the Upaniṣadic quotation

key_passages.why_this_passage: This compact exegetical move is representative because late Mādhva scholasticism repeatedly uses liminal states such as deep sleep and death to preserve asymmetrical dependence between jīva and Viṣṇu. It therefore displays Rāghavendra's ontology not as a bare assertion of difference, but as a disciplined reading of scriptural phenomenology.
key_passages.source_edition: Rāghavendra Tīrtha, Tantra Dīpikā, ed. R. S. Panchamukhi (1964); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/raghavendra_tantra_dipika_archive.pdf

key_passages.locus_long: Tantra-Dīpikā, adhyāya 1, pāda 4, discussion of the sūtra jñeyatvavacanāc ca
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanād ānumānikāder jñeyatvāvacanāc cety arthaḥ |
"tam evaikaṃ jānathātmānam" iti brahmaṇa eva mumukṣu-jñeyatva-vacanāt ||

pada_analysis.gloss: because of the statement that it is to be known by the seeker of liberation
from the inferential and the rest
because of the absence of any statement of knowability
because of the statement that it is to be known by the seeker of liberation

samasa_vigrahas.note: A collective expression for inferentially posited entities and the like.

karaka_structure.note: The quoted imperative commands knowledge of the self alone.
Appositional accusative specifying the same object.
The knowable belongs to Brahman alone, not to an inferential substitute.

key_passages.english_close: The meaning is this: because there is a statement that Brahman alone is the knowable for the seeker of liberation, and because there is no statement of knowability for what is merely inferential and the like. "Know that one Self alone": because there is a statement that Brahman alone is to be known by the seeker of liberation.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Liberating knowledge, on Rāghavendra's reading, must terminate in Brahman / Viṣṇu rather than in a merely inferred ultimate.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the status of being the proper object of knowledge
what is posited merely by inference

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is representative because Rāghavendra is not content to say that scripture is authoritative in the abstract; he specifies the liberating object of knowledge and excludes merely inferential ultimates from that role. That is a central late-Mādhva soteriological discipline, not a marginal scholastic nicety.
key_passages.source_edition: Rāghavendra Tīrtha, Tantra Dīpikā, ed. R. S. Panchamukhi (1964); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/raghavendra_tantra_dipika_archive.pdf
